qa_pairs.question: How are interpreted User-Defined Functions (UDFs) integrated into Ansys Fluent simulations on an Alliance cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: To integrate an interpreted UDF into an Ansys Fluent simulation, you must add a specific command line to your journal file before the case or data files are read or initialized. This command is `define/user-defined/interpreted-functions "sampleudf.c" "cpp" 10000 no`, where `sampleudf.c` should be replaced with your source file's name. The UDF source file must be in the same directory as the journal file, and any existing managed definitions in the case file should be removed by resaving it in the Fluent GUI. For parallel jobs, an interpreted UDF will also need to be parallelized.

qa_pairs.question: What is the process for using a compiled User-Defined Function (UDF) with Ansys Fluent on an Alliance cluster, and what are the key considerations?
qa_pairs.answer: To use a compiled UDF with Ansys Fluent, the UDF must first be compiled on an Alliance cluster to create a `libudf` subdirectory containing the `libudf.so` shared library. This `libudf` directory can then be transferred to other Alliance clusters if the same `StdEnv` module version is loaded. In your journal file, uncomment the `define/user-defined/compiled-functions load libudf` command to load the compiled library. It's crucial not to leave both the compile and load commands uncommented in your journal file for job submissions, as this will lead to inefficient recompilation for every job and potential build conflicts. Compiled UDFs also require parallelization for parallel jobs.

qa_pairs.question: Why is it necessary to parallelize a User-Defined Function (UDF) for Ansys Fluent parallel jobs, and where can researchers find detailed instructions for this process?
qa_pairs.answer: It is necessary to parallelize a User-Defined Function (UDF) for Ansys Fluent parallel jobs (both single-node Shared Memory Parallel (SMP) and multi-node Message Passing Interface (MPI)) to control how specific parts of the UDF code run across different processes. Failure to do so can result in slow performance or immediate crashes. The parallelization procedure involves adding compiler directives, predicates, and reduction macros to your serial UDF. Detailed instructions for this process can be found in "Part I: Chapter 7: Parallel Considerations" of the Ansys 2024 Fluent Customization Manual.
